message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #2 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: FeatureMatrix accepts non-numeric rows despite its numeric type contract
DIMENSION: public API & adapter-Protocol stability
SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:17-30 
DESCRIPTION: FeatureMatrix declares rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...] and the module docstring states output is 'a plain numeric FeatureMatrix ... so the engine consumes it without knowing what the features mean.' __post_init__ validates row COUNT and WIDTH but never that the cells are numeric. PoC verified: FeatureMatrix(unit_ids=('u1',), feature_names=('f',), rows=(('not-a-number',),)) is accepted. A FeatureBuilder adapter that emits strings/None therefore passes construction and run_study's ingest-time checks (which only validate unit_ids dedup/ghost ids, pipeline.py:330-340), letting non-numeric 'features' flow into any downstream numeric consumer and fail far from the source — a silent break on a minor adapter change, with the only contract signal being a type annotation the runtime ignores.
SUGGESTED FIX: Validate cell types in FeatureMatrix.__post_init__: reject any cell that is not a real, finite number (e.g. `if not isinstance(x, (int, float)) or isinstance(x, bool) or not math.isfinite(x): raise ValueError(...)`), mirroring the finite-float discipline already enforced in confirm._validate_finite and the canonical hashing path.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	"""Feature-builder interface — optional dataset-specific features.
2	
3	The baseline toolkit provides generic builders (embeddings, similarity, etc.). When a
4	dataset has features only meaningful in its own domain, an adapter supplies them through a
5	:class:`FeatureBuilder`. Output is a plain numeric :class:`FeatureMatrix` keyed by unit id,
6	so the engine consumes it without knowing what the features mean.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	from dataclasses import dataclass
12	from typing import Protocol, Sequence
13	
14	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
15	
16	
17	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
18	class FeatureMatrix:
19	    """Numeric features for a set of units. ``rows[i]`` corresponds to ``unit_ids[i]``."""
20	
21	    unit_ids: tuple[str, ...]
22	    feature_names: tuple[str, ...]
23	    rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...]
24	
25	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
26	        if len(self.rows) != len(self.unit_ids):
27	            raise ValueError("FeatureMatrix row count must match unit_ids length")
28	        width = len(self.feature_names)
29	        if any(len(r) != width for r in self.rows):
30	            raise ValueError("FeatureMatrix rows must match feature_names width")
31	
32	
33	class FeatureBuilder(Protocol):
34	    """Build dataset-specific numeric features from a canonical corpus."""
35	
36	    def build(self, corpus: Corpus) -> FeatureMatrix:
37	        """Compute features for (a subset of) the corpus units. Must be deterministic."""
38	        ...
39	
40	    @property
41	    def provides(self) -> Sequence[str]:
42	        """Names of the features this builder produces (for provenance)."""
43	        ...
44

content.text: The finding is accurate on the code. `__post_init__` validates only row count and width, not cell numeric-ness. Let me verify the PoC and check downstream consumption claims.

print('ACCEPTED None:', fm2)
fm3 = FeatureMatrix(unit_ids=('u1',), feature_names=('f',), rows=((float('nan'),),))
print('ACCEPTED nan:', fm3)
"

content.text: PoC confirmed: strings, None, and NaN are all accepted. The pipeline validation (lines 330-339) only checks unit_ids dedup and ghost ids, never cell types. Now let me check how feature_matrices are consumed downstream and whether the codebase enforces finite-float discipline elsewhere (the suggested-fix precedent).

content.text: Now let me check what `feature_matrices` actually feeds into. The key question is whether they reach a numeric consumer. Lines 175 and 575 show they're stored on a result object and returned. Let me check the consumer side and primitives.py for how baseline FeatureMatrix is built (to see if non-numeric is reachable from the blind builder path vs. only adapter path).

163	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
164	class StudyResult:
165	    """The reproducibility package: the artifacts + the append-only trail that produced them."""
166	
167	    study: str
168	    corpus_fingerprint: str
169	    baseline: BaselineArtifact

177

180	    """A typed metric-logging callback for :func:`run_study`'s best-effort tracker."""
181

184

186	
187	
188	def _now() -> _dt.datetime:
189	    # Ordering instants are a security/methodology check and ALWAYS use real wall-clock — never

1	"""Dependency-light, deterministic numeric primitives — the baseline's raw material.
2	
3	These are the small, exact, NumPy-free building blocks the methodology calls "raw material"
4	for a baseline: similarity/distance and descriptive statistics over a
5	:class:`~assay_engine.contracts.features.FeatureMatrix`. They are deterministic (no RNG, no
6	iteration-order dependence) and pure, so a study can assemble a simple baseline — and the
7	engine can unit-test these invariants — with no heavy dependency.
8	
9	Studies needing performance or richer ML (embedding models, clustering, graph/topology) bring
10	their own builders implementing :class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineBuilder`; the
11	engine deliberately does not prescribe those choices (ADR-0002). Heavier engine helpers may be
12	added later under the optional ``baseline`` extra, but the contract here stays dependency-free.
13	"""
14	
15	from __future__ import annotations
16	
17	import math
18	from typing import Any, Sequence
19	
20	Vector = Sequence[float]
21	
22	
23	def _check_same_length(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> None:
24	    if len(a) != len(b):
25	        raise ValueError(f"vectors must have equal length; got {len(a)} and {len(b)}")
26	
27	
28	def _check_finite(*vectors: Vector) -> None:
29	    """Reject non-finite components. Without this a NaN/inf could flow into cosine and be
30	    silently clamped to a false 1.0 (audit #B6); consistent with descriptive_stats."""
31	    for vec in vectors:
32	        for x in vec:
33	            if not math.isfinite(x):
34	                raise ValueError(f"vector components must be finite; got {x}")
35	
36	
37	def dot(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
38	    _check_same_length(a, b)
39	    return math.fsum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
40	
41	
42	def l2_norm(a: Vector) -> float:
43	    return math.sqrt(math.fsum(x * x for x in a))
44	
45	
46	def _clamp_unit(x: float) -> float:
47	    """Clamp to [-1, 1] to absorb floating-point overshoot (e.g. self-similarity 1.0000…002)."""
48	    return max(-1.0, min(1.0, x))
49	
50	
51	def cosine_similarity(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
52	    """Cosine similarity in [-1, 1]. A zero vector has no direction, so its similarity to
53	    anything is defined as 0.0 (rather than NaN). The result is clamped to [-1, 1] so float
54	    rounding cannot push it outside the documented range (audit #B3)."""
55	    _check_same_length(a, b)
56	    _check_finite(a, b)
57	    na, nb = l2_norm(a), l2_norm(b)
58	    if na == 0.0 or nb == 0.0:
59	        return 0.0
60	    return _clamp_unit(dot(a, b) / (na * nb))

content.text:
Now I have a clear picture. Key findings:

1. `FeatureMatrix` is consumed in `pipeline.py` only at ingest (build, validate ids, record provenance) and then stored on `StudyResult.feature_matrices` and returned. It does NOT flow into the baseline/methodology numeric path within the engine — the comment at pipeline.py:322-323 explicitly says "features are provenance/output, not a baseline input here."

2. The numeric primitives (`primitives.py`) that DO consume vectors have their own `_check_finite` guard (lines 28-34), so even if a study assembled a baseline from a FeatureMatrix's rows, the primitives would reject non-finite values — but NOT non-numeric types like strings (`math.isfinite('not-a-number')` raises TypeError, not a clean ValueError).

Let me verify the consumer behavior and check that the type contract is genuinely violated vs. defended.

# And the canonical hashing / serialization path — does FeatureMatrix get serialized anywhere?
"

content.text: Now let me check the canonical_json path — the provenance trail records `feature_names` (line 346) but not the rows themselves. Let me confirm the rows never reach canonical serialization, and check the `_canonical` module's float handling for context on the "canonical hashing path" the assessor referenced.

content.content:
1	"""Type-faithful canonical hashing — the engine's single content-digest primitive.
2	
3	Two unrelated places need a *stable, type-faithful* content hash: the baseline determinism
4	harness (so the same inputs reproduce the same baseline) and pre-registration (so a lock
5	binds the exact hypothesis content). Both must distinguish values that merely share a
6	``str()`` — ``int 1`` from ``str "1"``, ``date(2020,1,1)`` from ``"2020-01-01"`` — or the
7	hash is a liability rather than a guarantee.
8	
9	This module is the one implementation, hoisted here so it sits *below* both
10	``methodology`` and ``baseline`` in the import graph (neither may depend on the other). It is
11	dependency-free and pure. Anything it cannot canonicalize reproducibly is refused **loudly**:
12	a default (address-based) ``repr`` would make the digest differ every process and silently
13	break the guarantee it exists to provide.
14	"""
15	
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	import datetime as _dt
19	import decimal
20	import hashlib
21	import json
22	import uuid
23	from typing import Any, Mapping
24	
25	
26	def keytag(key: Any) -> str:
27	    """A type-faithful, sortable string for a mapping key, so distinct keys that happen to
28	    share a ``str()`` (e.g. int ``1`` vs str ``"1"``) do NOT collide."""
29	    return f"{type(key).__name__}::{key!r}"
30	
31	
32	def canonical_plain(value: Any) -> Any:
33	    """Canonicalize ``value`` into a fully JSON-native, type-faithful structure for hashing.
34	
35	    Mappings become a sorted list of ``[typed-key, value]`` pairs; bytes, sets, and any
36	    non-JSON-native leaf are tagged with their type rather than stringified. Nothing is coerced
37	    with ``str()``, so the content hash distinguishes values that share a ``str()``.
38	    """
39	    if value is None or isinstance(value, (str, bool, int, float)):
40	        return value  # JSON-native scalars; json encodes 1, "1", true, 1.0 distinctly
41	    if isinstance(value, bytes):
42	        return {"__bytes__": value.hex()}
43	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
44	        pairs = sorted(
45	            ([keytag(k), canonical_plain(v)] for k, v in value.items()), key=lambda p: p[0]
46	        )
47	        return {"__map__": pairs}
48	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
49	        return [canonical_plain(v) for v in value]
50	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
51	        return {"__set__": sorted((canonical_plain(v) for v in value), key=repr)}
52	    # A small allowlist of leaf types with a value-faithful canonical form.
53	    if isinstance(value, (_dt.date, _dt.datetime, _dt.time)):
54	        return {"__temporal__": value.isoformat()}
55	    if isinstance(value, decimal.Decimal):
56	        return {"__decimal__": str(value)}
57	    if isinstance(value, uuid.UUID):
58	        return {"__uuid__": str(value)}
59	    # Anything else is refused LOUDLY rather than repr-tagged: a default (address-based) repr
60	    # would make the digest differ every process, silently breaking the guarantee.
61	    raise TypeError(
62	        f"cannot canonicalize a leaf of type "
63	        f"{type(value).__module__}.{type(value).__qualname__!r} reproducibly — convert it to a "
64	        "JSON-native value, bytes, date/datetime/Decimal/UUID before hashing"
65	    )
66	
67	
68	def canonical_json(value: Any) -> str:
69	    """The canonical JSON text of ``value`` — stable across processes and key orderings."""
70	    return json.dumps(canonical_plain(value), sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False)
71	
72	
73	def hash_bytes(data: bytes) -> str:
74	    return hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
75	
76	
77	def hash_text(text: str) -> str:
78	    return hash_bytes(text.encode("utf-8"))
79	
80	
81	def hash_value(value: Any) -> str:
82	    """Stable, type-faithful content hash of an arbitrary value.
83	
84	    ``canonical_plain`` canonicalizes everything to JSON-native form first, so ``json.dumps``
85	    needs no ``default=`` coercion — anything it still cannot encode is a real bug to surface,
86	    not to silently stringify.
87	    """
88	    return hash_text(canonical_json(value))
89

content.text:
Now I have full context. Let me assess.

**The contract violation is real but its blast radius is narrow.** The annotation `rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...]` and docstring "plain numeric FeatureMatrix" promise numeric cells, and `__post_init__` never enforces it. PoC confirms strings/None/NaN accepted.

However, key mitigations:
- Within the engine, `feature_matrices` is **provenance/output only** (pipeline.py:322-323 comment), never fed into numeric primitives. So no in-engine numeric crash occurs.
- Rows are NOT serialized into the provenance trail (only `feature_names` at line 346), so the canonical hashing path isn't reached either.
- The numeric primitives that a *study-supplied* baseline might use already have `_check_finite` — but that defends finiteness, not type (a string would yield `TypeError: must be real number, not str`, a less clean failure).

So the assessor's "flow into any downstream numeric consumer and fail far from the source" is partially overstated for the engine itself, but valid for adapter/study code consuming `StudyResult.feature_matrices`. This is a genuine defense-in-depth / contract-integrity gap: a frozen, validated dataclass advertising a numeric type contract that the runtime doesn't enforce, in a codebase that elsewhere consistently enforces finite-float discipline (confirm.py, primitives.py, _canonical's `allow_nan=False`). The inconsistency is the real defect.

Let me check whether a NaN row would silently corrupt a hash if it ever did reach canonical_json (since `allow_nan=False`).

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
from assay_engine._canonical import canonical_json
# If FeatureMatrix rows ever reached canonical_json with NaN:
try:
    print(canonical_json([[float('nan')]]))
except Exception as e:
    print('NaN ->', type(e).__name__, e)
# A string would pass canonicalization (it's str-native) — silently treated as a 'feature'
print('str ->', canonical_json([['not-a-number']]))
"

content.content:
NaN -> ValueError Out of range float values are not JSON compliant: nan
str -> [["not-a-number"]]

content.text:
This confirms the subtle danger: if a non-numeric **string** ever reached canonical hashing, it would silently serialize as a legitimate value (`[["not-a-number"]]`) — corrupting a "type-faithful numeric" digest without any loud failure, exactly the kind of silent break the `_canonical` module's design explicitly tries to prevent. NaN fails loud, but strings pass silently.

I have enough to render a verdict.

**Verdict: TRUE, but MEDIUM is slightly generous — LOW-to-MEDIUM.** I'll settle on LOW given the constrained blast radius within the engine (provenance-only consumption, rows never hashed in the current path), while acknowledging it's a genuine contract-enforcement gap and consistency defect that the assessor correctly identified.

input.reasoning:
The factual claims are all verified. `FeatureMatrix.__post_init__` (src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:25-30) validates only row count and width, never cell type/finiteness, despite the `rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...]` annotation and the module docstring's "plain numeric FeatureMatrix" promise (lines 5-6, 19). PoC confirms `FeatureMatrix(unit_ids=('u1',), feature_names=('f',), rows=(('not-a-number',),))`, plus None and NaN variants, are all accepted. The pipeline's only feature-side ingest guards (pipeline.py:330-339) check unit_id dedup and ghost ids, not cell types. So an adapter FeatureBuilder emitting strings/None/NaN passes construction and ingest.

This is a genuine production-readiness defect: a frozen, runtime-validated contract dataclass advertises a numeric type that the runtime does not enforce, in a codebase that elsewhere consistently enforces finite-float discipline (confirm._validate_finite at confirm.py:87-88; primitives._check_finite at primitives.py:28-34; _canonical.canonical_json uses allow_nan=False). The inconsistency is the defect — the contract signal is a type annotation Python ignores.

However, MEDIUM overstates the blast radius. Within the engine, feature_matrices is provenance/output ONLY (explicit comment pipeline.py:322-323: "features are provenance/output, not a baseline input here"); it is stored on StudyResult.feature_matrices (pipeline.py:175,575) and returned, and is NOT fed into any in-engine numeric primitive, nor are the rows serialized into the provenance trail (only feature_names is recorded, pipeline.py:346). So the assessor's "flow into any downstream numeric consumer and fail far from the source" applies to adapter/study code that consumes StudyResult.feature_matrices, not to the engine's own numeric path. The numeric primitives a study-built baseline might use already reject non-finite values via _check_finite (though a string yields a less-clean TypeError, not a domain ValueError).

One concrete latent risk worth noting: I verified that if a string cell ever reached the canonical hashing path, canonical_json([["not-a-number"]]) silently serializes it as a valid value, corrupting a digest the _canonical module explicitly designs to be type-faithful and to refuse non-canonicalizable leaves loudly — a silent integrity break. NaN, by contrast, fails loud (allow_nan=False). This reinforces that early cell-type validation is the correct, codebase-consistent fix, but the current path does not reach hashing, so it stays latent.

Net: real, correctly identified, suggested fix is appropriate and consistent with existing patterns; severity recalibrated to LOW (defense-in-depth / contract-integrity gap with constrained current-day blast radius), not MEDIUM. Recommend implementing the suggested __post_init__ cell-type+finiteness check (rejecting bool, non-(int|float), and non-finite), as it is cheap, prevents a silent break on a minor adapter change, and matches the engine's finite-float discipline.
input.evidence:
PoC (verified): `FeatureMatrix(unit_ids=('u1',), feature_names=('f',), rows=(('not-a-number',),))` accepted; None and NaN variants also accepted.
src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:25-30 — __post_init__ checks only len(rows)==len(unit_ids) and width==len(feature_names); no cell validation. Annotation rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...] (line 23); docstring "plain numeric FeatureMatrix" (lines 5-6,19).
src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:322-340 — feature ingest: build, dedup-id and ghost-id guards only; comment lines 322-323 state features are provenance/output, not a baseline input. pipeline.py:346 records feature_names only (not rows). pipeline.py:175,575 — feature_matrices stored on StudyResult and returned, no numeric consumption in-engine.
Existing finite-float discipline the fix should mirror: src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:87-88; src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:28-34; src/assay_engine/_canonical.py:70 (allow_nan=False).
Latent integrity risk (verified): canonical_json([["not-a-number"]]) -> '[["not-a-number"]]' (silent), whereas canonical_json([[nan]]) raises ValueError. A string cell would corrupt a type-faithful digest silently if it ever reached hashing; it currently does not.

content.text:
Verdict: TRUE finding, recalibrated to LOW.

The assessor's factual claims are all correct and PoC-verified: `FeatureMatrix.__post_init__` (src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:25-30) validates only row count and width, never that cells are numeric/finite, despite its `tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...]` annotation and "plain numeric FeatureMatrix" docstring. Strings, None, and NaN all pass construction and the pipeline's ingest guards (which only check unit_id dedup/ghost ids at pipeline.py:330-339).

I downgraded MEDIUM to LOW because the in-engine blast radius is narrower than described: feature_matrices is provenance/output only (explicit comment at pipeline.py:322-323), never fed into the engine's numeric primitives, and the rows are not serialized into the provenance trail (only feature_names). The "fail far from the source" risk lands in adapter/study code consuming `StudyResult.feature_matrices`, not the engine itself.

It remains a genuine defect worth fixing: a runtime-validated frozen contract advertising a numeric type the runtime ignores, inconsistent with the finite-float discipline enforced everywhere else (confirm.py, primitives.py, _canonical's `allow_nan=False`). I also verified a latent integrity wrinkle — a string cell would silently serialize through canonical_json as a valid value, whereas NaN fails loud — reinforcing the assessor's suggested fix as correct and codebase-consistent.
